sound_type: physical percussive wake -- V-shaped rooster tail spray produced by swimming at up to 35 mph (55 km/h) just below the water surface; not a biological vocalization but the acoustic signature of extreme speed; E=6
ecosystem: Open water -- Puget Sound, Salish Sea, North Pacific deep channels; resident year-round
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Global population ~1.2 million. Japanese commercial hunting reached 40,000-42,000/year in peak 1980s-90s. Threats: bycatch, vessel strike, PCB contamination, habitat degradation.
milestone: FIRST PHOCOENIDAE entry in engine at degree 281. Porpoise family distinct from Delphinidae: spade-shaped teeth, rounded melon, no beak, generally quieter. Fastest cetacean of its size class at 35 mph. Named for William Healey Dall (1845-1927), Smithsonian naturalist. Described by True 1885.

coordinates.description: Resident in Puget Sound and Salish Sea year-round. Common in deeper channels. Frequently observed bow-riding on ferries and vessels.

speed_data.context: Fastest cetacean of its size class. Sustained speed. Bottlenose dolphin 15-20 mph; orca 30-34 mph in bursts; common dolphin ~25 mph. Dall's Porpoise sustains 35 mph.
speed_data.hydrodynamic_adaptations: Extremely robust caudal peduncle; distinctive dorsal and ventral keel; muscular body form (265-485 lb / 120-220 kg at 5.5-7.5 ft / 1.7-2.3 m).
speed_data.rooster_tail_mechanism: Bow wave effect when swimming at speed just below surface pushes water upward and forward, creating V-shaped spray plume above waterline. Height proportional to speed. Primary long-distance identification feature.

acoustic_data.echolocation_frequency: Approximately 120-150 kHz -- higher frequency, narrower-band than dolphin echolocation (orca: 20-100 kHz). Entirely ultrasonic, inaudible to humans.
acoustic_data.vocalizations: Notably limited compared to dolphins. Does not produce rich social call repertoire of Delphinidae.
acoustic_data.primary_human_audible_event: Rooster tail: hiss and rush of displaced water at high speed; percussive splash of spray returning to surface; churning wake. Physical percussive wake, not biological vocalization.

nodes.title: First Phocoenidae -- Porpoise Family Enters Engine
nodes.description: Degree 281 is the first Phocoenidae entry. Family distinct from Delphinidae: spade-shaped teeth, rounded melon, no beak, generally quieter. Six species worldwide. Phocoenoides dalli is the fastest and most robustly built.

nodes.title: Rooster Tail -- Physical Percussive Wake
nodes.description: V-shaped spray produced when Dall's Porpoise swims at speed just below surface. Bow wave effect pushes water upward and forward. Height proportional to speed. Primary identification feature at distance. Sound: hiss, splash, wake churn.

nodes.title: 35 mph -- Fastest Cetacean of Size Class
nodes.description: Up to 35 mph (55 km/h) sustained. Morphological adaptations: robust caudal peduncle, dorsal/ventral keel, compact muscular body. Speed sustained, not burst-only. Other cetacean comparison: orca 30-34 mph bursts, bottlenose 15-20 mph.

nodes.title: Phocoenoides dalli -- Nomenclatural History
nodes.description: Named for William Healey Dall (1845-1927), Smithsonian naturalist and North Pacific explorer. Described by Frederick William True, 1885. Monotypic genus. Two subspecies: P. d. dalli (Dall-type, found in Puget Sound) and P. d. truei (True-type).

cross_references.relationship: Delphinidae at 224 dB vs. Phocoenidae at 35 mph. Both use echolocation; Dall's clicks are higher frequency (120-150 kHz) and narrower-band. Different families, different acoustic modes.
Delphinidae returns at 282. E=7. Aerial acrobatics and pod calls. Energy rises from Dall's efficient E=6 to dolphin's acrobatic E=7.
E=6 + E=6 = E=12. Deadpan punk precision paired with high-speed cetacean efficiency. Both operate with economy and exactness at E=6. Neither requires explosion to make its mark.
